q: HI: random vs sequential access on modern SSDs?
options: They're now equally fast on SSDs
Random access is still significantly slower than sequential, even on SSDs — a common misconception
Sequential is slower on SSDs than HDDs
SSDs eliminate the need for indexes
why: HI flags as a misconception that SSDs equalize access; random is still much slower than sequential (far worse on HDDs), making proper indexing critical.

q: HI: what cost does every index impose on writes?
options: Writes become impossible until the index is dropped
A single write may trigger several disk writes, since every index must also be updated
Indexes only cost space, never write speed
Writes get faster because the index caches the row
why: HI: on insert/update the DB updates the main table plus every index, so multiple indexes mean a single write triggers several disk writes, plus extra disk space.

q: Per HI, roughly what latency win does reading a profile from Redis give over Postgres?
options: About 5x (10ms to 2ms)
About 50x (50ms to 1ms)
About 2x (4ms to 2ms)
About 500x
why: HI: Postgres ~50ms vs Redis ~1ms, a 50x improvement, because memory sits closer to the CPU and avoids disk.

q: HI: the primary risk of write-behind (write-back) caching?
options: Writes get very slow (both cache and DB must confirm)
If the cache crashes before flushing, you can lose data
It pollutes the cache with never-read data
Every read needs a directory lookup
why: Write-behind writes only to cache and flushes to the DB asynchronously — fast writes, but data loss if the cache crashes before flushing. Suits workloads tolerant of occasional loss.

q: HI: cache warming only prevents stampedes under which condition?
options: When using request coalescing
When you use TTL-based expiration (not invalidation on writes)
When the cache is in-process
When you replicate hot keys
why: HI: warming refreshes popular keys before they expire, which only helps with TTL-based expiration. If you invalidate on writes instead, warming doesn't prevent stampedes.

q: HI: the single most effective way to handle a cache stampede?
options: Increase TTL to several hours
Request coalescing / single-flight — one request rebuilds while others wait
Switch to write-through
Shard the cache across more nodes
why: HI calls request coalescing (single-flight) the most effective fix: one request rebuilds the entry while others wait, instead of hundreds hitting the DB.

q: Per HI, which statements about caching patterns/layers are correct? (select all)
options: Cache-aside is the interview default
Redis natively supports write-through out of the box
CDNs are a form of read-through cache
In-process caching is an optimization layer added AFTER an external cache
why: HI: cache-aside is the default, CDNs are read-through, in-process caching comes after external. It explicitly says Redis does NOT natively support write-through — app code/framework is needed.

q: HI: even powerful cloud databases max out around a storage ceiling that forces sharding. What number does HI cite for Amazon Aurora?
options: 256 GiB
2.5 TB
256 TiB
16 TB
why: HI: even Aurora maxes out around 256 TiB — the kind of ceiling that forces splitting data across machines.

q: HI: what distinguishes partitioning from sharding?
options: Partitioning spans machines; sharding stays on one
Partitioning splits data within a single DB instance; sharding splits across multiple machines
They're identical with no difference
Partitioning is NoSQL-only; sharding SQL-only
why: HI: partitioning usually means splitting within one instance (ranges/hash); sharding means across machines. Terms are used loosely — what matters is one machine vs many.

q: HI: why is is_premium (a boolean) a bad shard key?
options: Booleans can't be hashed
Only two values means at most two shards, with uneven load
It needs a directory lookup per request
It changes on upgrade, forcing resharding
why: HI's canonical bad shard key: low cardinality (two values) caps you at two shards, and if most users are free, one shard is overloaded. Good keys need high cardinality + even distribution.

q: HI: which sharding strategy will interviewers assume unless you say otherwise?
options: Range-based
Hash-based
Directory-based
Vertical partitioning
why: HI labels hash-based sharding the default and most common; it's what your interviewer assumes unless you explicitly state otherwise.

q: Per HI, which are valid ways to handle hot spots or cross-shard problems? (select all)
options: Isolate hot keys to a dedicated shard
Use compound shard keys like hash(user_id + date)
Default to two-phase commit for all cross-shard writes
Denormalize related data to keep it on one shard
why: HI recommends isolating hot keys, compound keys, and denormalizing. It warns AGAINST 2PC as the go-to (slow, fragile) — prefer sagas or avoiding cross-shard transactions.

q: HI: what core problem does consistent hashing solve vs simple modulo hashing?
options: It eliminates hot keys entirely
It minimizes data redistribution when adding/removing a node
It removes the need for a hash function
It guarantees strong consistency
why: HI: modulo hashing (%3 to %4) remaps almost every key; consistent hashing moves only a bounded fraction when membership changes.

q: HI hash-ring example: how do you find which database an event maps to?
options: hash(event_id) modulo node count
Hash the event ID to a point on the ring, then move clockwise to the next DB node
Look it up in a directory table
Assign to the lowest-load node
why: HI: place nodes on the ring, hash the event ID to a point, move clockwise to the next node — avoiding modulo and limiting movement on membership change.

q: HI: what problem do virtual nodes specifically solve?
options: Hot keys from popular content
When a node fails, its whole load dumps on its single clockwise neighbor, overloading it
The need for replication
Making the ring larger than 2^32
why: HI: without virtual nodes a failed node's load shifts entirely to one neighbor (doubling it). Virtual nodes place each DB at many ring points so failure load spreads evenly.

q: Per HI, which statements about consistent hashing in real systems are correct? (select all)
options: Virtual nodes fix structural imbalance; replication/key-salting fix workload imbalance
Redis Cluster uses consistent hashing with virtual nodes
Redis Cluster uses fixed hash slots — 16,384 via CRC16(key) mod 16384
DynamoDB replicates each partition across 3 AZs, so a primary failure promotes a replica with no data movement
why: HI: virtual nodes fix key-distribution imbalance while replication/salting fix traffic imbalance; Redis Cluster uses fixed slots (NOT consistent hashing); DynamoDB replicates partitions across 3 AZs so failover moves no data.

q: HI: CAP theorem practically reduces to which single choice?
options: SQL vs NoSQL
Consistency vs availability when a network partition occurs
Shard vs replicate
Strong vs eventual durability
why: HI: partition tolerance is non-negotiable in any distributed system, so CAP boils down to — prioritize consistency or availability when a partition occurs?

q: HI USA/Europe profile example during a partition: which choice and why?
options: Consistency — a stale name is unacceptable
Availability — showing the old name beats showing an error
Consistency — profiles are financial data
Reject both reads and writes until healed
why: HI: for the profile example the answer is clear — show the stale old name (availability) rather than an error; a stale name beats no name.

q: Per HI, which should prioritize CONSISTENCY over availability? (select all)
options: Ticket/seat booking (prevent double-booking)
Viewing a Yelp restaurant's hours
E-commerce inventory (prevent overselling)
Stock trading order books
why: HI lists ticket booking, inventory, and financial/trading as consistency-required. Yelp showing slightly outdated hours is an availability example — brief staleness is fine.

q: HI: the model where users always see their own updates immediately, but others may see older versions, is…
options: Causal consistency
Strong consistency
Read-your-own-writes consistency
Eventual consistency
why: HI: read-your-own-writes — common in social media so users see their own profile edits right away, even if others briefly see stale data.

q: HI: the default consistency model of most distributed databases when prioritizing availability?
options: Strong consistency
Causal consistency
Eventual consistency
Read-your-own-writes
why: HI: eventual consistency is the most relaxed model and the default of most distributed databases — what you implicitly choose when prioritizing availability.

q: HI: a common giveaway that a candidate has only book knowledge is…
options: Not knowing CAP
Doing scale math with outdated hardware numbers (2015/2020) that underestimate modern systems
Failing to mention sharding
Using Redis for caching
why: HI: relying on outdated hardware constraints leads to over-engineered designs — one of the biggest tells of book-only knowledge.

q: HI 2026 numbers: roughly how much RAM does a high-memory instance like AWS U-24tb1.metal provide?
options: 512 GiB
4 TB
24 TB
60 TB
why: HI cites U-24tb1.metal at 24 TB RAM (X1e.32xlarge 4 TB, M6i.32xlarge 512 GiB) — many apps that once needed distributed systems now fit on one machine.

q: HI network latency tiers: approximate latency across availability zones within the same region?
options: Sub-1ms
1-2ms
why: HI: sub-1ms within a single AZ, 1-2ms across AZs in a region, 50-150ms cross-region.

q: HI: how is object storage like S3 characterized as a constraint?
options: Limited to ~60 TB per bucket
Effectively unlimited — petabyte-scale is standard practice
Capped at 256 TiB like Aurora
Only for cold archival data
why: HI: S3 is effectively unlimited (petabyte-scale standard); the days of storage being a primary constraint are largely behind us.

q: Which 2026 hardware figures does HI specifically cite? (select all)
options: i3en.24xlarge ~60 TB local SSD
D3en.12xlarge ~336 TB HDD
Within a datacenter, 25 Gbps is common
A single Postgres instance should be sharded at 100GB
why: HI cites i3en.24xlarge 60 TB SSD, D3en.12xlarge 336 TB HDD, 25 Gbps datacenter networking. It explicitly criticizes the textbook 'split at 100GB' as obsolete.

q: HI: what core problem is Elasticsearch presented as solving?
options: Durable message streaming between services
Full-text search and querying over large volumes of data
Strongly consistent transactional writes
Distributed locking and coordination
why: HI frames Elasticsearch around search/querying over big data via inverted indexes — not a queue, lock service, or transactional store.

q: HI: the relationship between an Elasticsearch index, shards, and Lucene?
options: An index is a single Lucene file shared across nodes
An index is split into shards, and each shard is a self-contained Lucene index
Shards are read replicas of one master Lucene instance
Index and shard are two names for the same thing
why: HI: an index is partitioned into shards for horizontal scaling, and each shard is itself a complete Lucene index that can live on a different node.

q: HI: why must you choose the number of primary shards carefully up front?
options: Primary shard count can't change without reindexing into a new index
Adding primary shards auto-rebalances at zero cost
Shard count only affects read latency
Elasticsearch picks it automatically, unconfigurable
why: HI: primary shard count is fixed at index creation; changing it requires reindexing into a new index — a common gotcha interviewers probe.

q: HI: the common anti-pattern with Elasticsearch is using it as…
options: A search layer alongside a primary DB
The single primary datastore / source of truth
A tool for autocomplete and fuzzy matching
A store backed by inverted indexes
why: HI: Elasticsearch should generally not be your source of truth — keep a separate primary DB and sync ES as a secondary search index.

q: Per HI, valid ways to keep Elasticsearch in sync with a primary DB? (select all)
options: Change Data Capture (CDC) streaming DB changes
Application-level dual writes to both stores
A periodic full table scan as the only real-time mechanism
Clients writing directly to ES, bypassing the DB
why: HI presents CDC and dual writes as the standard sync strategies. A periodic scan isn't real-time sync, and bypassing the primary DB contradicts the source-of-truth pattern.

q: HI: which structure makes Elasticsearch efficient for full-text search?
options: A B-tree keyed on document ID
An inverted index mapping terms to the documents containing them
A consistent hash ring
A skip list of timestamps
why: HI: the inverted index maps each term to the documents containing it, enabling fast full-text search, unlike a B-tree keyed on IDs.

q: HI Kafka deep dive: how is a topic distinguished from a partition?
options: A topic is physical storage; a partition is a logical label
A topic is a logical grouping; a partition is the physical, ordered append-only log
They're identical concepts
A partition can span multiple topics
why: HI: a topic is a logical grouping of messages; a partition is the physical ordered immutable append-only log. A topic can have many partitions across brokers.

q: HI: how does a Kafka consumer group ensure each event is normally processed once?
options: Every consumer reads every partition and deduplicates
Each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group
Kafka randomly load-balances each message
The producer decides which consumer handles each message
why: HI: within a consumer group each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer, so under normal operation each event goes to a single consumer.

q: HI single-broker capacity estimate on good hardware?
options: ~100GB data and 100K msgs/sec
~1TB data and ~1M msgs/sec
~10TB data and 10M msgs/sec
~500GB data and 50K msgs/sec
why: HI's hand-wavy but useful estimate: a single broker stores ~1TB and handles up to ~1M messages/sec — used to decide whether scaling is even needed.

q: HI: how is a Kafka message routed to a partition by default?
options: partition = hash(key) % num_partitions using murmur2
Round-robin regardless of key
By timestamp modulo partition count
By the broker with the lowest load
why: HI: partition = hash(key) % num_partitions with murmur2 (the default). A bad key causes hot partitions.

q: Per HI, strategies for handling a hot partition (e.g. the Nike LeBron ad)? (select all)
options: Random salting of the partition key
Compound key such as ad ID plus region
Storing the full payload blob directly in Kafka
Back pressure to slow the producer
why: HI lists no-key default partitioning, random salting, compound keys, and back pressure. Storing large blobs in Kafka is an anti-pattern, not a fix.

q: HI: which feature does Kafka NOT natively support that AWS SQS does?
options: Message ordering within a partition
Built-in consumer retries and dead letter queues
Durable replication across brokers
Offset tracking for consumers
why: HI: Kafka has no out-of-the-box consumer retry support — you build your own retry topic + DLQ. That's why the Web Crawler breakdown chose SQS, which provides them natively.

q: HI: the core, primary function of an API Gateway that candidates often forget?
options: Authentication
Request routing to the appropriate backend service
Response caching
SSL termination
why: HI: candidates emphasize middleware but forget the core reason — request routing: deciding which backend handles each incoming request.

q: HI request flow: when does the gateway perform request validation?
options: After routing to the backend
Before anything else, as the first step
Only after caching the response
Never — validation is the backend's job
why: HI lists validation first, so malformed requests (bad JSON, missing API key) are rejected early without wasting backend resources.

q: HI: the two load-balancing dimensions around an API Gateway?
options: Client-to-gateway via a dedicated LB (ELB/NGINX); gateway-to-service done by the gateway itself
The gateway always handles client-to-gateway balancing internally
Load balancing is never associated with gateways
Backend services balance themselves; the gateway never does
why: HI separates client-to-gateway balancing (a dedicated LB like ELB/NGINX in front) from gateway-to-service balancing (the gateway distributing across backend instances).

q: HI: what responses are appropriate to cache at the API Gateway?
options: Highly user-specific responses to personalize per user
Frequently accessed data that changes infrequently and is NOT user specific
Every response — caching is always safe
Only error responses
why: HI: gateway caching fits frequently accessed, infrequently changing, non-user-specific data where the same input returns the same result.

q: HI bottom line: when to introduce an API Gateway?
options: Always, even for a simple client-server app
For microservices; skip it for a simple monolithic / single-client architecture
Only when you need a database cache
Only for systems with fewer than three services
why: HI: use a gateway with microservices, but it's overkill for simple monolithic or single-client systems.

q: HI: how should you handle the API Gateway when speaking in an interview?
options: Detail its full middleware pipeline
Briefly say it handles routing and basic middleware, then move on
Replace it with a message queue
Model it as the source of truth
why: HI stresses the gateway is thin and not the interesting part; the bigger risk is over-spending time — note routing + middleware and move on.
